main_summary.summary: Rev. Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker, interviewed June 20, 2014 in Chester, Virginia by David Cline, recounts his trajectory from a New Jersey preacher's-kid childhood through eight years as pastor of Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg, Virginia, to his service as SCLC executive director under Martin Luther King Jr. Walker describes integrating the Petersburg ministerial alliance and the Petersburg public library (February 27, 1960), which he characterizes as the first library desegregated in the South, and the arrest that followed. He details building SCLC from a $57,000 budget and one office into a $1,000,000 budget and 100 staff, his design and execution of Project C in Birmingham, the year of advance planning before demonstrations, the strategic use of the Huntley-Brinkley evening news, the typing of King's Letter from the Birmingham Jail from marginalia on newspapers and toilet paper, and his role as Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington. Theresa Walker joins to recount the Mother's Day 1963 Birmingham assault and a subsequent arrest with four children in East Point, Georgia. Walker also describes his Harlem pastorate at Canaan Baptist Church, anti-apartheid organizing tied to the ANC, and charter school advocacy in New York with Rev. Floyd H. Flake.
key_themes: Project C and the Birmingham campaign
Letter from Birmingham Jail transcription and naming
Black church infrastructure as movement foundation
main_summary.historical_significance:
Walker's testimony is a first-person operational account from inside SCLC's executive leadership during the campaigns that produced the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965. He provides direct attribution of Project C's design, the year of advance reconnaissance in Birmingham (counting eating-facility seats, timing walks by age cohort), the deliberate cultivation of Bull Connor's overreaction, and the choreography of the children's marches and fire-hose footage to reach the Huntley-Brinkley audience.

The interview preserves Walker's claim of authorship-by-transcription on Letter from the Birmingham Jail and his naming of the document, his pre-King fantasy of plastic-bomb retaliation against Theodore Bilbo and James Eastland, and Theresa Walker's eyewitness account of the Mother's Day 1963 Gaston Motel violence. Walker's late-career anti-apartheid mobilization and charter school activism extend the record beyond 1968.

chapters.title: New Jersey Childhood and the Color Line
chapters.summary: Walker introduces himself as born in Brockton, Massachusetts, August 16, 1928, raised as a preacher's kid in a South Jersey congregation. He recounts his father's Greek and Hebrew study, his first encounter with segregation when a white neighborhood playmate told him she couldn't play with him because he was colored, segregated schools, Klan cross-burning at a Father Divine headquarters, and growing up with Frederick Douglass on the wall in what he calls Little Georgia.
related_events: segregated schooling in northern New Jersey
Klan cross-burning at Father Divine headquarters

chapters.title: Call to Ministry and Virginia Union
chapters.summary: Walker describes majoring in physics and chemistry intending to be a physician, then turning to ministry because of his experiences with segregation in Richmond, Virginia. He attended Virginia Union University and its graduate school of religion, earned his MDiv, and was called at age 22 (later corrected to 23) to Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg, where he served eight years. He states the Black preacher is the freest man in society because he is accountable to the Black community.

chapters.title: Petersburg Library Desegregation
chapters.summary: Walker recounts integrating the Petersburg ministerial alliance with the Virginia State College chaplain and R.G. Williams, then trying eight years later to integrate the public library. After the NAACP declined to take the case, he and Williams led a demonstration on February 27, 1960, entered through the white door, were arrested, and chose to stay in jail. He requested volume one of Douglas Southall Freeman's biography of Robert E. Lee. He characterizes it as the first time a library was desegregated in the South.

chapters.title: Meeting King and Nonviolence
chapters.summary: Walker dates his joining King's organization to 1952, serving on the board and writing press releases. He recounts carrying a Smith & Wesson nickel-plated .32 to Petersburg and putting it down under King's influence, his college-era fantasy of plastic-bomb retaliation against Theodore Bilbo and James Eastland, and his father's train-station encounter with a Norfolk-area constable in which the father had a coat-pocketed pistol ready to fire. Walker dates his SCLC board entry to the 1958 Clarksdale, Mississippi meeting.
related_events: King's call the day before assassination

chapters.title: Building SCLC
chapters.summary: Walker describes SCLC at his arrival in 1960 as mainly an idea in King's briefcase. He installed a fundraising system, took the budget from $57,000 to $100,000 in year one, and to a $1,000,000 budget with 100 full-time employees within four years. He brought two Petersburg staff with him, growing SCLC from one to five staff initially. He places money as the ammunition needed to train people for nonviolence, which was a novel concept in 1960 and 1961.
related_events: SCLC succession from Ella Baker as interim executive director

chapters.title: Albany Lessons
chapters.summary: Walker contests the media verdict that Albany was a failure, naming two problems: too many targets and SNCC having initiated the campaign before inviting King. He describes the inter-organizational warfare with CORE, NAACP, and SCLC, attributes Louis Lomax's 'tail between his legs' verdict to telephone interviews from a journalist who never came to Albany, and credits Albany with teaching SCLC how to mobilize an entire community for a campaign, without which Birmingham would not have been won. He references Charles Melvin Sherrod, whom he had raised in his Petersburg congregation and helped finance with a Woolworth grant.

chapters.title: Project C and Birmingham
chapters.summary: Walker recounts being assigned by King to design the Birmingham campaign, going to Birmingham a year ahead of time to measure walking times by age cohort and count seats in eating facilities. The plan, named Project C for confrontation, was unanimously accepted by the SCLC executive committee. Bull Connor's resistance produced the TV pictures, and the children's march toppled the segregation system. Walker describes 43 nights of mass meetings, the bus company strike in the first week, and crediting the Public Accommodations title to Birmingham.
related_events: Public Accommodations title of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

chapters.title: Media Strategy and the Huntley-Brinkley Report
chapters.summary: Walker describes the deliberate cultivation of three to four minutes nightly on the Huntley-Brinkley report. After a delayed afternoon demonstration produced a thousand onlookers rather than participants, he flew back to Atlanta to tell King they had to slow down marches and let Bull Connor escalate with dogs and water hoses. The strategy put SCLC on the evening news two or three nights in a row. He recounts the Black church infrastructure as the foundation that made the mass rallies religious in character.

chapters.title: March on Washington and Civil Rights Legislation
chapters.summary: Walker recounts being named Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington and SCLC plans to extend Project C to Danville, Virginia, which was shelved after King's assassination. He attributes the Public Accommodations Act of 1964 to Birmingham, and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 to Selma. He describes his role typing King's Letter from the Birmingham Jail from marginalia on newspaper edges and toilet paper across two or three nights, naming the document (the Quakers had first called it Tears of Love), and his judgment that it equals the Gettysburg Address.
related_events: Letter from the Birmingham Jail publication

chapters.title: Leaving SCLC for Harlem
chapters.summary: Walker describes leaving SCLC because the board froze his salary at $10,000 with a wife and four children while he administered a $1,000,000 budget. He took a $25,000 marketing vice president position with a New York publisher producing a series on Black life. Rev. Martin Luther King Sr. came to his Atlanta home to try to persuade him to stay. Walker remained King's man in New York and a representative to the UN, and went to jail with King in Birmingham in 1967 for parading without a permit, when the two photographed each other in the cell.

chapters.title: Anti-Apartheid and Charter Schools
chapters.summary: Walker describes post-1968 work on residual racism in New York, his immersion in the charter school movement alongside Rev. Floyd H. Flake and Rubén Díaz Sr., and opening the first charter school in New York with Steve Klinsky. He recounts ANC recruitment for anti-apartheid organizing, mobilizing a 300-member church federation, traveling to South Africa with 22 ministers, working on Mandela's presidential election, and the theological grounding for the work in the New Testament. He discusses King's intellectual sources (Buber, Walden Pond, Thoreau, Tillich) and the imitation-of-Christ paradigm, and rejects post-racial framing after Obama's election.

chapters.title: Memphis and Movement Reflections
chapters.summary: Walker addresses the Memphis campaign, attributing the disrupted march to the Invaders being put up to it by the FBI, and his belief that King had called him the day before the assassination to come to Memphis. He discusses federal complicity in disrupting the movement, particularly the Kennedy administration, and King's late-career focus on jobs and the economy as outgrowths of racial hatred. He closes by noting Vernon Johns's maxim that the truth is always inflammatory.

chapters.title: Theresa Walker on Mother's Day 1963 and East Point
chapters.summary: Theresa Walker recounts the Saturday before Mother's Day 1963: King asked Walker to stay in Birmingham after the bombing of A.D. King's home; Theresa brought the four children; an Alabama National Guardsman struck her in the head at the Gaston Motel when she went back for two sleeping children. Driving home to Atlanta, she was arrested with her five children in East Point, Georgia. Rev. Martin Luther King Sr. arrived at midnight with a property-owning bail underwriter. She was fined $84 at trial, refused to pay, was re-jailed, and Robert J. Brown paid the fine. Walker simultaneously was beaten with batons at a motel, sustaining a broken wrist, three fractured ribs, and a dislocated spine. Theresa was a freedom rider and was the only wife of a movement icon in their cell of 14 women in Mississippi. Their daughter, barred from Petersburg public schools after they refused to sign the pupil-placement form, was homeschooled, finished Howard in three years, did a three-year master's at Yale, and became deputy director of the National Endowment for the Arts.

chapters.title: Closing Reflections and Maya Angelou
chapters.summary: Walker closes by stating he would change nothing, that the 1960s were an exciting time to be alive, and that the Lord has removed thoughts of vengeance from his heart. He notes Maya Angelou worked on his staff, running the SCLC fundraising office in New York on 125th Street, and references her meeting King at a Harlem rally where Walker introduced them. He cites her line about being too young for wisdom and too handsome for virtue from I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings.
